words: GRACE WICKS: I want to talk first about Mary Jane Lamphear. She was a maiden
lady, born in 1843,in Verona, New York. And she never considered herself
a New Englander. She was an upper York Stater. Her next, she was the
oldest of the family of Richason and Mary Roberts Lamphear."In later
studies of the family history we learn that it was spelled with an 'ui'
rather than an ?H!. And with various endings, such as ier, and ere. But
this family always carried the ear syllable'.She was the oldest of three
children, her parents having been married 1841. They lived on the same
home which still is in the family. I believe the last owner I knew of to
have it was Blair Lamphear, in that location. The family were farmers
and cheese makers. Mary Jane, as the oldest of the family, I should say
that her next brother was Joshua Giles, my grandfather, and then sometime
later, a son Edgar Roberts Lamphear was born. As the oldest of the family,
Mary Jane was givena very great deal of the household resonsiblities. Her
mother not being particularly well. The story was told about the mother arr
almost 60, being completely casted. She had her feet in the oven door
with a little rocker drawn up in front of the kitchen range. And was
greatly babied by the whole family. She wore a shawl and a little cap.
Because of her advanced age, which of course was about 57.
And the meals
of the family were prodigious. Of course they did a great deal of outdoor
work in a cold climate time and they could handle a great many
strona oods. But pie for breakfast was very usual, on top of a very
substantial breakfast, which included meat and potatoes always. The
pies were made ahead of time and stored on the shelf of either the milk
room or the outdoor cellar. These remained cold both summer and winter.
And there would be maybe twenty pies in a row and they would be the
substantial kind,AMince, pumpkin, apple and so forth. And those were brought
in. from time to time to be sliced and eaten. Auntie, as she was known
to us children was the main cook and the main manager of the household.
She was most unusual in that she was a businesswoman throughout her life.
And took care of herself very adequately indeed. With some very distinct
periods in her career. The first thing I know of that she did was to
be a Yankee young woman who sold bibles south of the Mason-Dixon line
immediatley following the Civil War. She met much hostility as a down east
Yankee, they didn't call her a 'damn yankee'but they did call her a down
east Yankee. And the most dramatic incident of this hostility was when
a man set his dog upon her. She had no defense but a tiny, little, pearl
handled pen knife, which I have, in the pocket of her many folded skirt.
She put her hand to this, but of course, there was no opportunity to open
the blade and the beast came roaring at her, and put his two great paws
right up on her shoulders and his face right in hers. But she managed to
stand her ground and the dog did nothing of hurting her. And the man who
owned the dog was utterly amazed.I've always wondered whether she made a
sale with this impressed gentleman or not.(laughs)At any rate, I don't
remember if I heard whether she ever made a sale at that place or not.
But she did make a living. Selling bibles in this very difficult situation.
As time went by, her needlework, which throughout her life was an art became
her source- of livlihood. And I don't know when the periods were.I do know
that through her long years she had dressmaking establishments in Toronto,
in Cincinnati and in Chicago, and I believe, though I'm not sure of this,
in Marquette, Michigan. One time or another. She had a large workroom of
women who helped her and at one time she had a designer named Van Buren
who was a Hollander,who worked for her. The fashion of the day was very
elaborate and materials were very costly, being woven only eighteen inches
wide.
Being very heavy in quality and costing a great deal. Twenty
dollars a yard was not unknown. With the dollar at that stage of the game,
you can imagine what a garment cost especially^designing, drafting the
designing and execution of the dressmaking establishment. Auntie's name,
M.J. Lamphear was on an embroidered label which went pnto the back of the
garment. And if a woman, in those days, of quite a wealthy man, had two
dresses of this quality during her lifetime, she was considered very
fortunate. The materials were of exquisite quality. I have a silk quilt made
of their scraps. Of course, now they are well over a hundred years old.
And those are intact and beautiful in every way. It's only after we got to
synthetic materials which had metal woven in them had tin woven in them,
ect., that materials cracked after a period of time. But these materials
of Auntie's were, are, in beautiful shape.As are the silks,which were
pure silk, that made the threads that held them together in their fine
and delicate stitches. I know that my great aunt was in business in 1869
because I have a bound volume of patterns from the Petersons Magazine of
that year. They were bound by a cousin of hers named Roberts in New York
City. And for my wedding present my mother gave me the May and the
February fold out pictures, which I have framed on my wall. Together with
a beautiful piece of crewel work in shades of green and gold and off white
in a water lily pattern. Those three framed delights have been a precious
part of my home ever since I have been married. Which now going back to
1929, makes it 45 years.

speaker: SS
words: Would you describe the design of the dresses of that time.

speaker: GW
words: Well, we think we have nice clothes now, we think our French designers are

speaker: GW
words: very talented people. But as I look at these pictures as I sit here, they

speaker: GW
words: are exquisite in detail.And finished in design. The human being who wore

speaker: GW
words: them was greatly enhanced in dignity and beauty and she was a complete unit.

speaker: GW
words: Her waist must be very tiny. Her bosom must be ample. Her hips, it didn't

speaker: GW
words: matter, because the skirts flowed widely. And the ruffles were hand made

speaker: GW
words: and hand hemmed. The pleatings were done on a fluting iron. The puffs were

speaker: GW
words: done by hand. The gorgeous ribbons and laces were imported. Sometimes from

speaker: GW
words: Italy, sometimes from Belgium, sometimes from France. The jet was hand

speaker: GW
words: cut. The garments were looked at in the pattern books, which were numerous.

speaker: GW
words: And then the purchaser bought what she wished. These garments were

speaker: GW
words: elaborate evening wear, they were wedding wear, they were morning wear,

speaker: GW
words: for afternoon tea and calling complete with exquisite little hats. Lovely,

speaker: GW
words: dainty boots, and charming, warm, coats. The women's tasks in taking care

speaker: GW
words: of these clothes was par for the course for these women to have perhaps

speaker: GW
words: two outfits during their entire lifetime, of this quality. Because materials

speaker: GW
words: at that time were used and reused by turning the materials from right

speaker: GW
words: side to wrong side. Sometimes the wrong sides or the underside of the

speaker: GW
words: first side were quite as pretty as the outsides had been. And frequent

speaker: GW
words: practice for the garment to be turned and used almost as a new dress.

speaker: GW
words: Because of the new appearance it would have. Though of course the pattern

speaker: GW
words: would have to be much the same since the folds would be there, etc.

speaker: GW
words: Now they could press materials in those days, but they had no dry

speaker: GW
words: cleaning. So the grooming of a woman had to be very, very careful. And

speaker: GW
words: her only deodorant was talcum and since there was no dry cleaning, sponging

speaker: GW
words: had to be resorted to a great deal, because of course women did perspire.

speaker: GW
words: But of course women on this social level frequently the idea of culture

speaker: GW
words: and gentility was not to be vigorous physically. So they were very careful

speaker: GW
words: in their physical activities. And didn't (pause in tape) Itself is a work

speaker: GW
words: of art.

speaker: SS
words: The silk quilt.

speaker: GW
words: Uh huh. It is an exquisite piece of handwork. I am sorely tried in my

speaker: GW
words: decisions as to how to handle it to the greatest advantage. I would like

speaker: GW
words: to cut it into squares and frame it so that it would be under glass, the

speaker: GW
words: various parts, and give it to the descendents of my parents. But I hate

speaker: GW
words: to cut it up because it's such a beautiful thing and also because I am not

speaker: GW
words: sure of the care to which the framed pieces would be subjected. Because

speaker: GW
words: unless people are taught that old examples of a culture which is now

speaker: GW
words: gone by will be properly handled and properly appreciated. Then again,

speaker: GW
words: I think, but it belongs to these people. And they have a right to it

speaker: GW
words: and I would be so happy to give it to them if I thought they would treat

speaker: GW
words: them with loving respect. This is a decision I have to make before my

speaker: GW
words: life ends. I have taken a sample or so of the materials on the lower

speaker: GW
words: shelf there of that exquisite little flower piece, which after all these

speaker: GW
words: many years, still shows the shadings of colors. Dyes were not as secure

speaker: GW
words: in those days as they are now. And light faded them. As a matter of fact,

speaker: GW
words: light fades our dyes presently.But those dyes in that little framed piece

speaker: GW
words: of embroidery still hold in delicate pinks and blues for the flowers and

speaker: GW
words: many shades of green for the leaves. I framed that in a gold leaf frame,

speaker: GW
words: because I wanted it to be kept as the exquisite thing it is. There are

speaker: GW
words: many other samples in this partly finished quilt that I could also

speaker: GW
words: give and I have to make this decision.

speaker: SS
words: Did the women of Genesee have dresses like the ladies of the larger cities?

speaker: GW
words: My mother did. But hers was a hand me down from this aunt. Women in Genesee

speaker: GW
words: had dresses they made themselves. Or at best, had a dressmaker make. Now

speaker: GW
words: my grandfather, Barbara Walters Lamphear, the sister-in-law of the woman

speaker: GW
words: about whom I have been telling you,was a pioneer seamstress. And I have a

speaker: GW
words: beautiful lion glass compote which was given her in 1886. As a thank you

speaker: GW
words: for having made a trouseau in Michigan for a friend whoA she didn't charge.

speaker: GW
words: And so in gratitude, this lovely piece of glassware was given her, and

speaker: GW
words: that now graces my electric organ. And from time to time I put different

speaker: GW
words: flowers in it to enhance it's pleasures and right now it has poinsettas in

speaker: GW
words: it. But the pioneer women in Genesee, unless something was sent to them .

speaker: GW
words: did not have this quality of clothing.

speaker: SS
words: Would that be true of Moscow as well?

speaker: GW
words: Of course, yes. Because there were no conditions which allowed an establish

speaker: GW
words: ment that cost such as this one did.It had to be in a large city area.

speaker: GW
words: Because this was the top drawer, Well now to continue about Miss

speaker: GW
words: Lamphear, she was engaged to a gentleman in Chicago, at one time, and

speaker: GW
words: they were to be married. He took a little trip down into Illinois for

speaker: GW
words: business and failed to come back. And her dismay put her on a train to

speaker: GW
words: go down to see what had happened. And she found a raw grave. He had been

speaker: GW
words: attacked by some dreadful throat infection which killed him forthwith. And

speaker: GW
words: since she was afiance^ there was no information in his effects to notify

speaker: GW
words: her. And so she learned what had happened, but never did she marry. And

speaker: GW
words: I presently wear this diamond, which is about a 4/5ths carat that was her

speaker: GW
words: engagement ring from this gentleman. As she grew older, the joints of her

speaker: GW
words: ring finger grew large with rhuematism. So she couldn't wear the ring,

speaker: GW
words: but as long as I can remember seeing Auntie undress, on her corset was

speaker: GW
words: pinned a little satin sack and in it was this diamond ring which was her

speaker: GW
words: dearest possesion. I also have another piece of lovely jewelry which

speaker: GW
words: perhaps was not her, a gift from her beloved.

speaker: GW
words: But was typical of the

speaker: GW
words: time in that they didn't have bifocal glasses. She wore glasses, but she

speaker: GW
words: had to have heavier glasses with which to read. And so I have this

speaker: GW
words: lovely gold pin, that I presently wear which has under it a hook and

speaker: GW
words: on this hook she put the nose piecej which was gold^of her reading glasses.

speaker: GW
words: And carried it on her person, usually on her left shoulder and left

speaker: GW
words: shoulder area, and from that extended a gold chain with ay little chip

speaker: GW
words: diamond in it. I got this fixed, ready to wear down at the Dodson's

speaker: GW
words: Jewelers recently, because it's in style presently and of course is of

speaker: GW
words: such exquiste quality I'm very proud of it. But she always wore this

speaker: GW
words: as a means of being able to read wherever she was in her busy day. ACause

speaker: GW
words: she'd have to hook on the heavier lenses in order to be able to read a

speaker: GW
words: recipe or a label on a fruit jar or whatever it was, as she moved around

speaker: GW
words: her work. In due time, about the turn of the centuryyshe came west and

speaker: GW
words: joined her brother Joshua and his wife Barbara Walters, who I just mentioned

speaker: GW
words: in Troy,Idaho. And since grandfather was a locater of people on claims,

speaker: GW
words: helped her to get a claim of timber 18 miles into the tall,tall timber

speaker: GW
words: out of Orofino. The easy access, easier acess timber claims around

speaker: GW
words: Bovill were long since taken.And so her holding was much more inconvenient.

speaker: GW
words: But I have here a picture of her dn this claim. And she is in this log

speaker: GW
words: cabin with a breeze way in one end and one room for living purposes.

speaker: GW
words: And as usual}there are graces about her life. She has climbing cucumbers

speaker: GW
words: and climbing sweet peas at the lower end of her house and in her yard

speaker: GW
words: and at the northern part of it, she has a, some little trees started

speaker: GW
words: which she is put a stake beside it and is getting ready to grew. Outside

speaker: GW
words: alsoAis the washtub and the washboard where she kept tidy. And as she

speaker: GW
words: leans against her chair, it is a lovely old antique rocker of solid oak,

speaker: GW
words: covered with a tapestry back and cushion. As she leans on it now I can

speaker: GW
words: see that the light catches on the lenses of her reading glasses. She

speaker: GW
words: undoubtedly wearing hers, although that is not visible. Whether she had

speaker: GW
words: on her usual glasses or not is not shown in the picture from sure she was.

speaker: GW
words: As she leans on the back of this rocking chair you can see her nice hands.

speaker: GW
words: She always had her hair curled, she always was dressed properly when

speaker: GW
words: anyone saw her in the morning times. And she always had a certain grace

speaker: GW
words: about the food she served and the way in which it was consumed. She was

speaker: GW
words: a delight to know, and many of the small graces which came into my life

speaker: GW
words: were due to her. Oh yes, I see down by the doorstep that she has pie plant,

speaker: GW
words: rhubarb,growing.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know that cabin was built?

speaker: GW
words: That was hired done. She made herself as a concession to her outdoor life,

speaker: GW
words: a pair of very large bloomers that came clear down midcalf. And these protected

speaker: GW
words: her in her heavy work around the place. And she had chickens, but no cow.

speaker: GW
words: And I have a letter of hers which thanks the family for sending her butter

speaker: GW
words: and other supplies. I'm sure she was very low in the financial position

speaker: GW
words: at this time and the brother and his wife are subsidising her quite

speaker: GW
words: sure of this.

speaker: SS
words: Did she ever express her feelings about leaving the city life to come to

speaker: SS
words: such a rural place?

speaker: GW
words: Of course she did, but this is not recorded. And I was such a tiny little

speaker: GW
words: girl that I don't remember, so I would be fabricating that, so I shant

speaker: GW
words: mention it. Undoubtedly there was deep heattache in this. And the reason

speaker: GW
words: I have heard, that the reason that she came west was that she had finacial

speaker: GW
words: difficulties because she trusted people who betrayed her trust. She lent

speaker: GW
words: money particularly to the girls who worked in her workroom who were

speaker: GW
words: unable to pay.^It was just one of those things that you couldn't fight.

speaker: GW
words: And so, it was to her loss. Now I also see that she has these garments on

speaker: GW
words: as she's standing there. Her waist, however, is one with a puffed sleeve

speaker: GW
words: at the shoulder. She has on one of her exquisite white lace collars of

speaker: GW
words: which I found several the other day in a box of hers.She crocheted them.

speaker: GW
words: She tatted lace for them and she bought them from importers. But they

speaker: GW
words: were perfectly beautiful, and she had a little tie at the center of them

speaker: GW
words: in front and I never saw her without a lovely little coller on the top

speaker: GW
words: of her dress, around her neck.

speaker: GW
words: Auntie was the kind of person who took care of people. No one took

speaker: GW
words: care of Auntie, but - took care of people. When we would have a major

speaker: GW
words: illness, she would come and help. When my parents wanted to go to the

speaker: GW
words: World's Fair in Seattle in 1910, she came and took care of us children

speaker: GW
words: on our farm.At that time I was four years old, almost. And I remember

speaker: GW
words: being with her every place and how I adored her. And she carried me,

speaker: GW
words: large child that I probably was that time, she carried me avery great

speaker: GW
words: deal, and I remember sitting on her lap.and playing with her sweet cheek

speaker: GW
words: which was always soft and lovely to kiss. And burying my nose under her

speaker: GW
words: left ear. And smelling the good smell of her talcum and her person.

speaker: GW
words: To me, she was the very essense of protection and warmth, acceptance and

speaker: GW
words: comfort. I remember another incident which shows Auntie's particular flavor.

speaker: GW
words: On this farm where we lived, which is at the top of Coyote Grade over in

speaker: GW
words: Nez Perce county, where I was born,in 1906, and now this was 1910, so of

speaker: GW
words: course I can remember, we went out to get material to start the fire.

speaker: GW
words: And always dad or the big boys had wood cut and kindling cut and usually

speaker: GW
words: they brought it into the house. But this time, for some reason,we went after

speaker: GW
words: kindling. And in and out of the stack of wood, there darted a weasel. Auntie

speaker: GW
words: just walked right up to that pile of wood and plucked that weasel forth

speaker: GW
words: by theyback of his head^ took another piece of kindling and dispatched

speaker: GW
words: him forthwith. Now for any human being to be fast enough to catch a weasel

speaker: GW
words: is unheard of. But to catch him by the back of his head where he couldn't

speaker: GW
words: hurt her was out of this world for dexterity, aggressiveness and completeness

speaker: GW
words: of the human being involved.(laughs) But this she did. She also always

speaker: GW
words: harnassed and drove her own team, which were special to her. Another

speaker: GW
words: incident of Auntie's Orofino days^ I must not fail to tell, was of her

speaker: GW
words: going to the spring for water, which was outside her yard, and carrying

speaker: GW
words: home the water, two buckets of it. When in her path,,appeared the sweetest

speaker: GW
words: little cub bear. She set down one bucket, scooped the bear up into her

speaker: GW
words: apron and made for the house. The mother bear was right upon her. So she

speaker: GW
words: had to drop the baby, get inside the gate, which she made just in time.

speaker: GW
words: But her intent was to save this cute, little for us Jain children.

speaker: GW
words: Course, it's just as well she didn't make it. That was what she was

speaker: GW
words: thinking of and what she was bold enough to try. Another thing that was

speaker: GW
words: charming her at this interlude,some one said to her,"Miss Lamphear,

speaker: GW
words: weren't you afraid way off by yourself like that?" She said,"Afraid? Afraid

speaker: GW
words: of what?" She said,"My white old hairs would frighten anybody away and I

speaker: GW
words: can prove It, because one night I looked up from reading before I went to

speaker: GW
words: bed and there was a man's face framed in my window. I went to the door

speaker: GW
words: and called and called but he run away and never came near me.(laughs)

speaker: GW
words: Now wouldn't that be the response of a completely individual human being?

speaker: GW
words: She was delightful.

speaker: GW
words: ...grandfather and his wife in their final illnesses. Grandmother died

speaker: GW
words: first and it was many, many days that Auntie didn't have her clothes off

speaker: GW
words: to sleep. She just bathed and changed clothes and kept up so that she

speaker: GW
words: could tend to her sister-in-law at any hour of the day or night. She

speaker: GW
words: died of a combination of Brightfs disease and diabetes and it was a very

speaker: GW
words: trying illness, but Auntie, who was older than she by at least six years

speaker: GW
words: managed to do it. Then there was a sweet and peaceful interlude where

speaker: GW
words: whe and her brother enjoyed great companionship in Troy. And from time

speaker: GW
words: to time she sold off the holdings on her timber claim, They lived very

speaker: GW
words: comfortably in nice little house which still stands, In Troy. At

speaker: GW
words: night there was a very good light in the dining room and under that would

speaker: GW
words: go the rocker. And along side was the nice heating stove. And Auntie

speaker: GW
words: would establish herself there and read the daily paper from cover to

speaker: GW
words: cover. Out loud, so that her brother in the kitchen? who's eyes were

speaker: GW
words: sadly impaired could sit in his little chair, which he had bought for his

speaker: GW
words: mother when he was about 16 years old whichAit along toward 1850, and

speaker: GW
words: which still is in the family in the home of my brother, Ben Jain in

speaker: GW
words: Lapwai, he would sit in that little chair, which was a rocker, but which

speaker: GW
words: had long since had the rockers worn off eo flat from use, that it was a

speaker: GW
words: stationary chair. It was of white hardwood, kind, I couldn't tell you

speaker: GW
words: for sure and it had been used so long that it had a certain satiny look

speaker: GW
words: from the hands and the clothing that had touched it. Grandfather, whom

speaker: GW
words: we called Josh, always sat in that chair in the kitchen window. At a

speaker: GW
words: lovely which he held dear, and listened while Auntie read him the paper.

speaker: GW
words: This was a nightly ritual. And one in which I shared when I went to visit

speaker: GW
words: them. They had a fancy light in the middle of the room. It didn't shed

speaker: GW
words: the power that the one right behind the chair had. And lots of times I'd

speaker: GW
words: sit on Auntie's lap while she read. Or sometimes I'd be tucked up on the

speaker: GW
words: couch on the other side of the stove while she read. But however it was

speaker: GW
words: it was a complete, warm, comforting loving occassion, which we all

speaker: GW
words: enjoyed. And of course, we'd all be digesting one of her super suppers.

speaker: GW
words: Dinner was at noon, but we had steak and potatoes just as often for

speaker: GW
words: breakfast and for supper as we did for noon. We had three generous meals.

speaker: GW
words: And grandfather ate with his knife, And it was worn almost to a point

speaker: GW
words: from long years of use. Because it had been part of the silver of his

speaker: GW
words: parents' wedding household equipment. And his teeth had long since

speaker: GW
words: somewhat vanished. So that he had one tooth north and one tooth south, one

speaker: GW
words: upper and one lower. But with that he was able to handle all of the vittals

speaker: GW
words: that came his way. And oh, such goodies as she cooked! For example, at

speaker: GW
words: breakfast, there would be sourdough hotcakes, thin as paper and delicious

speaker: GW
words: to the taste. And they/always imported some maple syrup from the east,

speaker: GW
words: because after all, the other syrup wasn't fit to eat. And they loved

speaker: GW
words: baked potatoes, and oh my, how I envied grandfather, the way that he could

speaker: GW
words: pile a nice bite of baked potato on knife. l4 did look so tasty. And

speaker: GW
words: I was/never as/ ne able to achieve this. As a matter of fact, Auntie would have

speaker: GW
words: slapped my hands if I had. As near as she ever slapped my hands which was

speaker: GW
words: breakfast oh my, how we did love the heavy clotted cream on corn flakes.

speaker: GW
words: Of course, the regular thing was cooked oatmeal which had been on the

speaker: GW
words: stove all the evening before. And was the best stuff you ever tasted,

speaker: GW
words: with all that good, thorough cooking *that heavy, heavy cream on it with A

speaker: GW
words: plenty of good sugar. Sometimes white a nd sometimes brown. It made a

speaker: GW
words: variety, to use both kinds. But oh how good those cornflakes were, and

speaker: GW
words: to this day, I try to put some top milk or half and half on 'em and

speaker: GW
words: see if they'll taste anywhere near that good, but they never do,(laughs)

speaker: GW
words: Another thing that was typical of that period when I yisited those two,

speaker: GW
words: were the treats that grna,dfa,ther bought me. We'd go to town, and of course,

speaker: GW
words: I never called him grandfather. He was Josh, Josh would invite, me to go

speaker: GW
words: to town with him and we'd walk over to the village, which was probably about

speaker: GW
words: four blocks and he would buy me both candy and gum at the same time for

speaker: GW
words: a treat. Well now in the Jain family, you were allowed candy or you were

speaker: GW
words: allowed gum, but goodness sakes, you never had both.

speaker: GW
words: Not so with Joshua! He would supply and flirt with a little girl. With all the treats

speaker: GW
words: she

speaker: GW
words: saw she wanted. And what was popular then, in the form of gum was a long

speaker: GW
words: sick, about the size of a pencil. was white wax with a slight sweentening

speaker: GW
words: in it. And it was wrapped^tissue like paper that had red markings on it.

speaker: GW
words: With a little flip at each end. After you chewed that, for about so long,

speaker: GW
words: though, it got so it was crumbly, so you knew it'd done it's full measure

speaker: GW
words: for you. And you threw it out then. But until that time, oh my, how that

speaker: GW
words: was treasured. Another treat from that interlude that I must tell were

speaker: GW
